0.5 mg/mL poly-D-lysine stock solution (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume PDL hydrobromide 0.5 mg/mL 5 mg H 2 O n/a 10 mL Note: Make 1 mL aliquots and store at -20 °C.

steps.title: Dissection buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Dissection buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume HBSS n/a 490 mL 1 M HEPES 10 mM 5 mL 100× pen-strep 1× 5 mL Note: Store at 4 °C.

steps.title: Papain dissociation solution (5 mL) Reagent...
steps.instruction: Papain dissociation solution (5 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Papain n/a 1× glass vial Dissection buffer n/a 5 mL 25 KU Benzonase nuclease n/a 0.75 μL Notes:

steps.title: Add 5 mL of dissection buffer...
steps.instruction: Add 5 mL of dissection buffer into the vial containing papain and gently swirl until the powder has dissolved completely. Transfer the solution into a 15 mL conical tube and add 0.75 μL of Benzonase nuclease to the solution.

steps.title: Make fresh papain dissociation solution right before dissecting the animal
steps.instruction: Make fresh papain dissociation solution right before dissecting the animal.

steps.title: Plating media (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Plating media (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume MEM n/a 440 mL FBS 10% 50 mL D-glucose 5 mg/mL 2.5 g NaHCO 3 0.2 mg/mL 0.1 g 10 mg/mL insulin 0.025 mg/mL 1.25 mL Transferrin 0.1 mg/mL 50 mg 200 mM L-glutamine 2 mM 5 mL 100× pen-strep 1× 5 mL Notes:

steps.title: To dissolve transferrin, add 2 mL...
steps.instruction: To dissolve transferrin, add 2 mL of incomplete MEM (no supplements added) to the 100 mg transferrin vial. Place the vial into a 37 °C water bath until the transferrin has dissolved into a clear, light brown solution. Take 1 mL (50 mg) of the solution and add to the MEM bottle.

steps.title: To dissolve D-glucose, add 2
steps.instruction: To dissolve D-glucose, add 2.5 g of D-glucose to the bottle of MEM and place in a 37 °C water bath for 30 min. Gently swirl the bottle every 10 min. Alternatively, D-glucose can be added to the bottle of MEM and allowed to dissolve at 4 °C overnight.

steps.title: Do not repeatedly freeze-thaw L-glutamine
steps.instruction: Do not repeatedly freeze-thaw L-glutamine. Make 5 mL aliquots and store at -20 °C. If thawed L-glutamine has precipitated, warm the solution for 1 min in a 37 °C water bath and mix. Do not leave L-glutamine in a water bath for longer than 1 min.

steps.title: Mix all supplements added to the...
steps.instruction: Mix all supplements added to the bottle of MEM and sterile filter through a vacuum-driven 0.22 μm CA membrane.

steps.title: Complete P3 primary cell Nucleofector ®...
steps.instruction: Complete P3 primary cell Nucleofector ® solution (22 μL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume P3 Nucleofector ® solution n/a 18 μL Supplement 1 n/a 4 μL Notes:

steps.title: Volumes described here are sufficient for one sample (i
steps.instruction: Volumes described here are sufficient for one sample (i.e., one nucleofection).

steps.title: Store according to the manufacturer’s directions
steps.instruction: Store according to the manufacturer’s directions.

steps.title: 200 μM JF 646 -HTL and...
steps.instruction: 200 μM JF 646 -HTL and JF 549 -HTL stock solutions (500 μL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume JF 646 -HTL or JF 549 -HTL 200 μM 100 nmol DMSO n/a 500 μL Note: Make 50 μL aliquots and store at -20 °C. Avoid exposure to light.

steps.title: JF 646 -HTL blocking solution (2...
steps.instruction: JF 646 -HTL blocking solution (2 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume 20 μM JF 646 -HTL 20 nM 2 μL 10 mg/mL CHX 50 nM 2.8 μL NbActiv4 n/a 2 mL Notes:

steps.title: Dilute the 200 μM JF 646...
steps.instruction: Dilute the 200 μM JF 646 -HTL and 100 mg/mL CHX stock solutions 1:10 in NbActiv4 before making the blocking solution.

steps.title: Make the solution immediately before use...
steps.instruction: Make the solution immediately before use in prewarmed NbActiv4 (37 °C).

steps.title: JF 549 -HTL labeling solution (2...
steps.instruction: JF 549 -HTL labeling solution (2 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume 20 μM JF 549 -HTL 10 nM 1 μL NbActiv4 n/a 2 mL Notes:

steps.title: Dilute the 200 μM JF 549...
steps.instruction: Dilute the 200 μM JF 549 -HTL stock solution 1:10 in NbActiv4 before making the labeling solution.

steps.title: Make the solution immediately before use...
steps.instruction: Make the solution immediately before use in prewarmed NbActiv4 (37 °C).

steps.title: Imaging buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Imaging buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume NaCl 150 mM 4.383 g 1 M CaCl 2 2 mM 1 mL 1 M MgCl 2 2 mM 1 mL KCl 5 mM 0.185 g HEPES 10 mM 1.192 g D-glucose 30 mM 2.702 g H 2 O n/a Final volume to 500 mL Note: Adjust to pH 7.4 with NaOH. Store at 4 °C. Laboratory supplies

steps.title: Dilute 0
steps.instruction: Dilute 0.5 mg/mL PDL stock solution 1:10 to a working concentration of 0.05 mg/mL in sterile Milli-Q H 2 O.

steps.title: Add 200 μL of 0
steps.instruction: Add 200 μL of 0.05 mg/mL PDL to the center of the glass coverslip of a MatTek glass-bottom dish.

steps.title: Using a pipette tip, gently spread...
steps.instruction: Using a pipette tip, gently spread the PDL to cover the edges of the coverslip.

steps.title: Incubate the MatTek dish with PDL...
steps.instruction: Incubate the MatTek dish with PDL for 2 h at room temperature inside a biosafety cabinet.

steps.title: Aspirate PDL from the glass coverslip...
steps.instruction: Aspirate PDL from the glass coverslip of the MatTek dish and wash the dish three times using 500 μL of sterile H 2 O for 5 min each wash. B. Preparation of primary rat hippocampal neuron culture Notes:

steps.title: All neuronal culture steps should be...
steps.instruction: All neuronal culture steps should be performed inside a biosafety cabinet. Use sterile technique to avoid contamination.

steps.title: Handle tissues and cells with care
steps.instruction: Handle tissues and cells with care. Do not pipette with excessive force.

steps.title: Plating media and NbActiv4 should be...
steps.instruction: Plating media and NbActiv4 should be prewarmed to 37 °C prior to use.

steps.title: Keep fresh papain dissociation solution on...
steps.instruction: Keep fresh papain dissociation solution on ice until 5 min before use.

steps.title: Hippocampal neurons were prepared from P0...
steps.instruction: Hippocampal neurons were prepared from P0 Sprague-Dawley rat pups (Charles River) according to institutional guidelines and protocol (IACUC protocol number 24-0258, protocol title: “Procurement of animal tissue for research applications”). Protocols for isolating the hippocampus from P0 rat pups have been described in detail elsewhere [22]. Note: We collect hippocampi from 2–4 rat pups and perform dissociation on all hippocampi in a batch.

steps.title: Once hippocampi are excised, cut each...
steps.instruction: Once hippocampi are excised, cut each hippocampus into 3–4 smaller pieces using a sterile razor blade.

steps.title: Remove papain dissociation solution from ice...
steps.instruction: Remove papain dissociation solution from ice and allow it to sit at room temperature for 5 min.

steps.title: Transfer dissected hippocampal tissue pieces to...
steps.instruction: Transfer dissected hippocampal tissue pieces to a 15 mL conical tube and briefly allow tissue pieces to settle at the bottom of the tube. Note: Create a wide-bore P1000 tip by cutting the end of a P1000 tip with sterile scissors. This wide-bore tip can be used to transfer the tissue.

steps.title: Carefully remove any solution from the...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove any solution from the conical tube using a P1000 pipette.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of papain dissociation...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of papain dissociation solution for approximately 6–8 pieces of hippocampi from two rat pups. Note: Scale up the volume of papain dissociation solution accordingly.

steps.title: Place the tube with the hippocampal...
steps.instruction: Place the tube with the hippocampal tissue into a 37 °C water bath for 30 min for digestion. Gently swirl the tube every 5–7 min to ensure the enzyme digests all surfaces of the tissue.

steps.title: After 30 min of digestion, remove...
steps.instruction: After 30 min of digestion, remove the tube from the water bath and briefly allow tissue pieces to settle at the bottom of the conical tube.

steps.title: Carefully remove papain dissociation solution using a P1000 pipette
steps.instruction: Carefully remove papain dissociation solution using a P1000 pipette.

steps.title: Add 1 mL of prewarmed plating media to the tube
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of prewarmed plating media to the tube.

steps.title: Using a P1000 pipette, gently triturate...
steps.instruction: Using a P1000 pipette, gently triturate the digested tissue 3–4 times.

steps.title: Add 5 mL of prewarmed plating...
steps.instruction: Add 5 mL of prewarmed plating media to the tube and allow large, undissociated tissue pieces to settle to the bottom of the tube for >1 min. Single cells, small cell clumps, and some tissue debris will remain in the supernatant.

steps.title: Prepare a 50 mL conical tube...
steps.instruction: Prepare a 50 mL conical tube with a 40 μm cell stainer and add 1 mL of prewarmed plating media to wet the membrane to allow cells to smoothly pass through the filter.

steps.title: Using the P1000 pipette, gently transfer...
steps.instruction: Using the P1000 pipette, gently transfer the cell supernatant and pass cells through the 40 μm cell stainer.

steps.title: Repeat steps B10–14 on the remaining...
steps.instruction: Repeat steps B10–14 on the remaining hippocampal tissue inside the 15 mL conical tube.

steps.title: After all hippocampal tissue has been...
steps.instruction: After all hippocampal tissue has been dissociated and filtered, centrifuge the cell suspension at 100× g for 8 min.

steps.title: Remove supernatant containing cell debris from...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant containing cell debris from the cell pellet, add 1 mL of prewarmed plating media, and gently dislodge the cell pellet from the tube.

steps.title: Add an additional 4 mL of prewarmed plating media
steps.instruction: Add an additional 4 mL of prewarmed plating media.

steps.title: Determine the cell density and viability of the cell suspension
steps.instruction: Determine the cell density and viability of the cell suspension. We only use cell preparations if viability is greater than 90%. Note: We use a Vi-CELL BLU cell viability analyzer according to the manufacturer’s instructions. When determining the viability and density of cells, inspect the images taken by the cell counter to ensure that the suspension consists mostly of single cells and not cell clumps or debris.

steps.title: Plate 50,000 cells at the center...
steps.instruction: Plate 50,000 cells at the center of each PDL-coated MatTek dish to be used.

steps.title: Add an equivalent volume of prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add an equivalent volume of prewarmed NbActiv4 to the cells and gently rock the MatTek dish to spread the cells onto the PDL-coated coverslip.

steps.title: Allow cells to adhere onto PDL...
steps.instruction: Allow cells to adhere onto PDL for at least 3 h in the incubator at 5% CO 2 and 37 °C.

steps.title: Gently add 2 mL of prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Gently add 2 mL of prewarmed NbActiv4 into the dish and return the dish to the incubator.

steps.title: Replace 1 mL of NbActiv4 in...
steps.instruction: Replace 1 mL of NbActiv4 in the dish with fresh, prewarmed NbActiv4 every 7 days until neurons are ready for use (after DIV10). C. Adeno-associated virus transduction of cultured rat neurons with plasmids to insert HaloTag into Gria1 Notes:

steps.title: HaloTag is inserted into GluA1 using homology-independent targeted integration (HITI)
steps.instruction: HaloTag is inserted into GluA1 using homology-independent targeted integration (HITI). This process involves transfecting or transducing a neuron with two plasmids:

steps.title: a donor plasmid that contains the...
steps.instruction: a donor plasmid that contains the coding sequence for HaloTag ( Figure 1A ). Cas9 will create a double-strand break in Gria1 and release the HaloTag coding sequence from the donor plasmid, after which HaloTag can be inserted into Gria1 by non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) ( Figure 1B , top). When expressed, GluA1 will be tagged with HaloTag in its amino-terminal domain (ATD) ( Figure 1B , bottom). Mature receptors can be labeled with HaloTag ligand that has been conjugated with fluorescent dyes or other molecules ( Figure 1C ).

steps.title: We recommend using adeno-associated virus (AAV)...
steps.instruction: We recommend using adeno-associated virus (AAV) to deliver the Cas9 (PX

steps.title: and HaloTag donor (px552-sg-gria1-HT) plasmids into neurons
steps.instruction: and HaloTag donor (px552-sg-gria1-HT) plasmids into neurons. AAV infection results in the highest knock-in efficiency (~2.6% of cells transduced with both plasmids contain a HaloTag knock-in [23]) and has significantly less toxicity when compared to nucleofection. AAVs can be pseudotyped with AAV2/9 and rh10 capsids for efficient transduction. We use AAV packaged by the Janelia Research Campus Viral Services. For the protocol, please see [23].

steps.title: Neurons can be infected with AAV...
steps.instruction: Neurons can be infected with AAV between 3 and 14 days in vitro (DIV 3–14). Infecting the neurons at DIV 3 results in the highest rate of infection, which decreases as neurons age in vitro.

steps.title: The addition of virus to neuronal...
steps.instruction: The addition of virus to neuronal cultures should be performed inside a biosafety cabinet. Use sterile technique to avoid contamination.

steps.title: NbActiv4 should be prewarmed to 37 °C prior to use
steps.instruction: NbActiv4 should be prewarmed to 37 °C prior to use. Figure

steps.title: Homology-independent targeted integration can be used...
steps.instruction: Homology-independent targeted integration can be used to insert HaloTag into Gria1 in cultured rat hippocampal neurons. (A) Constructs used to insert HaloTag into the genomic loci of Gria1 using homology-independent targeted integration (HITI). The donor construct (Donor) contains the DNA sequence coding for HaloTag (HaloTag) and the single guide RNA that will target Cas9 to Gria1 (sgRNA). Importantly, the HaloTag coding sequence is flanked by Gria1 sequences that will be targeted and cut by Cas9 (red bars). Cas9 is expressed from a second plasmid (Cas9). Black bars represent promoters driving the expression of Cas9 and the sgRNA. (B) Cas9 creates double-strand breaks in the genomic loci of Gria1 and releases HaloTag from the Donor. HaloTag can be inserted into Gria1 by non-homologous end joining (NHEJ). When expressed from the edited copy of Gria1 (Gria1-HT ), GluA1 will contain HaloTag inserted into its amino-terminal domain (ATD; GluA1-HT). SP, signal peptide; LBD, ligand-binding domain; TMD, transmembrane domain; CT, C-terminal tail. (C) Diagram of AMPAR containing a GluA1 subunit (blue) with HaloTag (green) inserted into the ATD (red). AMPARs are tetramers that can contain zero to four GluA1 subunits. As HaloTag is inserted into the ATD, it will sit on the extracellular side of the receptor. HaloTag can be labeled with fluorescent dyes that are conjugated to HaloTag ligand (HTL), such as JF 549 -HTL. (D) Representative confocal image of a cultured rat hippocampal neuron expressing GluA1 tagged with HaloTag and labeled with JF 549 -HTL (GluA1-HT-JF 549 ). Scale bar, 25 μm. (E) Representative widefield images of edited neurons expressing GluA1-HT labeled with JF 549 -HTL (GluA1-HT-JF 549 ) and a fluorescent neuron marker (in this case, miRFP670 driven by a synapsin promoter). Scale bar, 100 μm.

steps.title: Mix 1 × 10 10 genomic copies of rh10-PX551 (Cas
steps.instruction: Mix 1 × 10 10 genomic copies of rh10-PX551 (Cas

steps.title: and 1 × 10 10 genomic...
steps.instruction: and 1 × 10 10 genomic copies of rAAV2-px552-sg-gria1-HT (donor) into 50 μL of prewarmed NbActiv

steps.title: Add the entire volume of virus...
steps.instruction: Add the entire volume of virus in NbActiv4 directly into the media of the culture dish and gently swirl to mix.

steps.title: Return the culture dish to the...
steps.instruction: Return the culture dish to the incubator at 5% CO 2 and 37 °C until cells are ready for use. HaloTag + cells can sometimes be identified as early as 4 days after infection (i.e., DIV 7), but it may be necessary to wait 7 days after infection (DIV 10). D. Nucleofection (alternative to AAV infection) Notes:

steps.title: If AAVs of PX551 and px552-sg-gria1-HT...
steps.instruction: If AAVs of PX551 and px552-sg-gria1-HT are not available, plasmids can be used for transfection via nucleofection when neurons are extracted and cultured on P

steps.title: However, nucleofection-based HITI has relatively low knock-in efficiency (~1
steps.instruction: However, nucleofection-based HITI has relatively low knock-in efficiency (~1.7% of cells transfected with both plasmids will contain a HaloTag knock-in [23]) and high toxicity when compared to AAV-based HITI.

steps.title: All neuronal culture steps should be...
steps.instruction: All neuronal culture steps should be performed inside a biosafety cabinet. Plasmids and solutions should also be prepared inside a biosafety cabinet. Do not remove the lid of the Nucleocuvette ® plate outside of the biosafety cabinet to avoid contamination. Use sterile technique to avoid contamination.

steps.title: Handle cells with care, especially after nucleofection
steps.instruction: Handle cells with care, especially after nucleofection. Avoid applying excessive shearing force during pipetting.

steps.title: Warm complete P3 Nucleofector ® solution...
steps.instruction: Warm complete P3 Nucleofector ® solution to room temperature before use.

steps.title: Prewarm plating media and NbActiv4 to...
steps.instruction: Prewarm plating media and NbActiv4 to 37 °C prior to use.

steps.title: The number of cells and volumes...
steps.instruction: The number of cells and volumes of solutions described below are sufficient for one sample (one transfection). Scale up accordingly for the desired number of transfections.

steps.title: Prior to dissection, prepare a 1
steps.instruction: Prior to dissection, prepare a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube with the donor and Cas9 expression plasmids by pipetting 0.5 μg of PX551 and 0.5 μg of px552-sg-gria1-HT in a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube. Note: Dilute stock concentrations of plasmids to 0.5 μg/μL and then use 1 μL of each.

steps.title: After performing steps B1–19, calculate and...
steps.instruction: After performing steps B1–19, calculate and transfer 500,000 cells into a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube for transfection.

steps.title: Gently pellet cells at 90× g...
steps.instruction: Gently pellet cells at 90× g for 5 min at room temperature in a centrifuge.

steps.title: Carefully remove media from the cell...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove media from the cell pellet and resuspend cells in complete P3 Nucleofector ® solution (P3 Nucleofector ® solution plus supplement 1). Use 20 μL of complete P3 Nucleofector ® solution for every 500,000 cells.

steps.title: Transfer 20 μL of cells in...
steps.instruction: Transfer 20 μL of cells in complete P3 Nucleofector ® solution to the 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube containing 0.5 μg of PX551 and 0.5 μg of px552-sg-gria1-HT.

steps.title: Transfer the entire volume of cells...
steps.instruction: Transfer the entire volume of cells into one well of a 96-well Nucleocuvette ® plate and gently tap the plate to ensure that the cell suspension has settled at the bottom of the well without bubbles.

steps.title: Load the plate onto 4D-Nucleofector ®...
steps.instruction: Load the plate onto 4D-Nucleofector ® and transfect cells using code CU-

steps.title: Add 80 μL of plating media...
steps.instruction: Add 80 μL of plating media into the well and allow cells to recover by placing the plate into the incubator at 5% CO 2 and 37 °C for 5 min.

steps.title: Transfer the entire volume inside the...
steps.instruction: Transfer the entire volume inside the well (~100 μL) to 500 μL of prewarmed plating media in a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Plate 50,000 cells at the center...
steps.instruction: Plate 50,000 cells at the center of the PDL-coated MatTek dish.

steps.title: Add an equivalent volume of prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Add an equivalent volume of prewarmed NbActiv4 to help spread the cells onto the PDL coating.

steps.title: Allow cells to adhere onto PDL...
steps.instruction: Allow cells to adhere onto PDL for at least 3 h in the incubator at 5% CO 2 and 37 °C.

steps.title: Gently add 2 mL of prewarmed...
steps.instruction: Gently add 2 mL of prewarmed NbActiv4 to the dish and return the dish to the incubator.

steps.title: Replace 1 mL of NbActiv4 in...
steps.instruction: Replace 1 mL of NbActiv4 in the dish with fresh, prewarmed NbActiv4 every 7 days until neurons are ready for use (after DIV10). E. Labeling cultured neurons that express GluA1-HaloTag with HaloTag ligand conjugated with Janelia Fluor dyes Notes:

steps.title: At DIV 10 (7 days after...
steps.instruction: At DIV 10 (7 days after AAV infection or 10 days after nucleofection), neurons can be labeled with JF 646 - or JF 549 -HTL to examine whether Gria1 was edited with HaloTag.

steps.title: Prewarm NbActiv4 and imaging buffer to...
steps.instruction: Prewarm NbActiv4 and imaging buffer to 37 °C prior to use.

steps.title: Perform labeling and wash steps inside...
steps.instruction: Perform labeling and wash steps inside a biosafety cabinet and use sterile technique.

steps.title: Prepare 20 nM JF 646 -HTL...
steps.instruction: Prepare 20 nM JF 646 -HTL or 10 nM JF 549 -HTL in 2 mL of prewarmed NbActiv

steps.title: Aspirate most of the NbActiv4 from...
steps.instruction: Aspirate most of the NbActiv4 from the neuronal culture dish (save a small volume of NbActiv4 covering the cells on the coverslip) and replace with NbActiv4 containing JF 646 -HTL or JF 549 -HTL.

steps.title: Return the dish to the incubator for 30 min
steps.instruction: Return the dish to the incubator for 30 min.

steps.title: Aspirate NbActiv4 with JF 646 -HTL...
steps.instruction: Aspirate NbActiv4 with JF 646 -HTL or JF 549 -HTL and replace with 2 mL of prewarmed imaging buffer. Repeat this wash three more times to remove unbound dye from the culture dish.

steps.title: View cells under a microscope with...
steps.instruction: View cells under a microscope with a 10× or 20× objective. Once a positive cell has been identified, switch to a higher magnification objective to view the neuron in detail. JF 646 can be excited with a 640 nm laser line or LED light with filters appropriate for Cy

steps.title: JF 549 can be excited with...
steps.instruction: JF 549 can be excited with a 561 nm laser line or LED light with filters appropriate for TRITC. Edited neurons should have GluA1-HT labeling in the cell body and dendrites, and especially strong labeling in the dendritic spines where GluA1 accumulates ( Figure 1D ). Note: A neuronal transfection marker may be used to confirm that cells expressing GluA1-HT are neurons and to estimate the transfection/transduction efficiency ( Figure 1E ). For example, we use the fluorescent protein miRFP670 driven by the neuron-specific synapsin promoter. See Troubleshooting #4 for more details. F. Sparse labeling de novo synthesized GluA1-HaloTag using block-and-chase labeling protocol Notes:

steps.title: At DIV 14, cultured neurons are...
steps.instruction: At DIV 14, cultured neurons are sufficiently developed to easily identify healthy, spiny dendrites. Consequently, labeling and imaging are best performed between DIV 14 and

steps.title: Prewarm NbActiv4 and imaging buffer to...
steps.instruction: Prewarm NbActiv4 and imaging buffer to 37 °C prior to use.

steps.title: Perform labeling and wash steps inside...
steps.instruction: Perform labeling and wash steps inside a biosafety cabinet and use sterile technique.

steps.title: CHX is added to the blocking...
steps.instruction: CHX is added to the blocking solution to inhibit the synthesis of GluA1-HT during the blocking step. After all existing protein is blocked by JF 646 -HTL, protein synthesis is allowed to recover by removing CHX. During the chase step, only newly synthesized GluA1-HT will be labeled with JF 549 -HTL. Because the newly synthesized GluA1-HT makes up only a small fraction of total GluA1-HT, the JF 549 signal will be sparse enough for single-particle tracking. Figure

steps.title: Block-and-chase protocol to sparsely label GluA1-HaloTag
steps.instruction: Block-and-chase protocol to sparsely label GluA1-HaloTag. (A) Workflow to sparsely label GluA1-HT with Janelia Fluor dyes. (B) Representative widefield image of a dendrite from a cultured rat hippocampal neuron expressing GluA1-HT first labeled with JF 646 -HTL (block; GluA1-HT-JF 646 ) and then JF 549 -HTL (chase; GluA1-HT-JF 549 ). Scale bar, 10 μm.

steps.title: Prepare 2 mL of JF 646...
steps.instruction: Prepare 2 mL of JF 646 -HTL blocking solution in prewarmed NbActiv

steps.title: Aspirate most of the NbActiv4 from...
steps.instruction: Aspirate most of the NbActiv4 from the dish (save a small volume of media to cover cells on the glass coverslip) and replace with 2 mL of JF 646 -HTL blocking solution.

steps.title: Return the dish to the incubator for 1 h
steps.instruction: Return the dish to the incubator for 1 h.

steps.title: Aspirate blocking solution and replace with fresh, prewarmed NbActiv
steps.instruction: Aspirate blocking solution and replace with fresh, prewarmed NbActiv

steps.title: Repeat this wash three times to...
steps.instruction: Repeat this wash three times to completely remove unbound dye and CHX.

steps.title: Return the dish to the incubator...
steps.instruction: Return the dish to the incubator for 3 h to allow protein synthesis to recover. Note: Vary the duration of this recovery step to tune the labeling density of GluA1-HT by JF 549 -HTL. For example, allowing synthesis a greater time to recover will result in higher labeling density.

steps.title: Prepare 2 mL of JF 549...
steps.instruction: Prepare 2 mL of JF 549 -HTL labeling solution in prewarmed NbActiv

steps.title: Aspirate NbActiv4 and replace with 2...
steps.instruction: Aspirate NbActiv4 and replace with 2 mL of JF 549 -HTL labeling solution.

steps.title: Aspirate labeling solution and replace with...
steps.instruction: Aspirate labeling solution and replace with 2 mL of prewarmed imaging buffer. Repeat this wash three times to completely remove unbound dye.

steps.title: Return the dish to the incubator for 30 min
steps.instruction: Return the dish to the incubator for 30 min.

steps.title: Wash cells one more time with fresh, prewarmed imaging buffer
steps.instruction: Wash cells one more time with fresh, prewarmed imaging buffer. G. Imaging single GluA1-HaloTag-JF 549 particles Note: The following steps describe imaging particles using highly inclined and laminated optical sheet (HILO) microscopy [24]. Other types of microscopy may also be used, but should be optimized accordingly (see General note #7 ).

steps.title: Place MatTek dish with labeled cells onto the microscope stage
steps.instruction: Place MatTek dish with labeled cells onto the microscope stage. Note: Maintain a constant temperature of 37 °C during imaging using an environmental control chamber, as changes to temperature can alter the motion of particles.

steps.title: Using a 640 nm laser and...
steps.instruction: Using a 640 nm laser and filters appropriate for Cy5 (Cy5 channel), identify a healthy neuron that expresses GluA1-HT labeled with JF 646 -HTL (GluA1-HT-JF 646 + ). GluA1-HT-JF 646 + neurons will contain a strong signal in the dendrites, especially in the dendritic spines where GluA1 accumulates ( Figure 1D ). Note: Although edited neurons can be identified at 100× magnification, it may be easier to first identify a neuron of interest at 20× magnification and then change the magnification to 100×.

steps.title: Move the field of view to...
steps.instruction: Move the field of view to a healthy, spiny secondary dendrite on the GluA1-HT-JF 646 + neuron ( Figure 2B , block ).

steps.title: Adjust the Z position to approximately...
steps.instruction: Adjust the Z position to approximately find the center of the dendrite.

steps.title: View this field of view with...
steps.instruction: View this field of view with a 561 nm laser and filters appropriate for TRITC (TRITC channel). GluA1-HT-JF 549 should be sparsely labeled, and single particles readily distinguishable ( Figure 2B , chase ).

steps.title: If HILO is used, use the...
steps.instruction: If HILO is used, use the TRITC channel to find the incident angle and illumination direction that produces the best signal-to-noise ratio for single GluA1-HT-JF 549 particles.

steps.title: Switch back to the Cy5 channel...
steps.instruction: Switch back to the Cy5 channel and find another healthy, spiny dendrite on the same cell. Again, adjust the Z position to find the center of the dendrite.

steps.title: Briefly switch to the TRITC channel to view single particles
steps.instruction: Briefly switch to the TRITC channel to view single particles. The incident angle and direction may need minor adjustments to find the best signal-to-noise for GluA1-HT-JF 549 particles. Make these adjustments quickly to avoid bleaching the single GluA1-HT-JF 549 particles.

steps.title: Once satisfied, start timelapse imaging at 20–50 ms exposure times
steps.instruction: Once satisfied, start timelapse imaging at 20–50 ms exposure times. Note: We use a multi-camera setup to simultaneously image in the Cy5 and TRITC channels ( Figure 2B ; Video 1 ). If a multi-camera setup is not available, record a reference image in the Cy5 channel before starting the timelapse in the TRITC channel. The cameras used in our setup have a field of view of 512 × 512 pixels, with a pixel size of 160 nm. We image the center Z plane for 400–1,000 frames. However, imaging setups with finer Z sectioning may require a Z stack. Video

steps.title: Timelapse sequence of GluA1-HT-JF 549 after...
steps.instruction: Timelapse sequence of GluA1-HT-JF 549 after block-and-chase labeling with JF 646 -HTL and JF 549 -HTL in the dendrite of a cultured rat hippocampal neuron. Scale bar, 10 μm. Left: Existing GluA1-HT labeled with JF 646 -HTL (block; GluA1-HT-JF 646 ). Center: Newly synthesized GluA1-HT labeled with JF 549 -HTL (chase; GluA1-HT-JF 549 ). Right: GluA1-HT-JF 646 and GluA1-HT-JF 549 images merged. H. Tracking and characterizing single GluA1-HaloTag-JF 549 particles

steps.title: Convert image sequences to TIFFs with NIS-Elements or ImageJ
steps.instruction: Convert image sequences to TIFFs with NIS-Elements or ImageJ.

steps.title: Download SLIMfast, DiffusionSingle, TrackValidator, and HMM_Batch programs for MATLAB
steps.instruction: Download SLIMfast, DiffusionSingle, TrackValidator, and HMM_Batch programs for MATLAB.

steps.title: Open MATLAB and create paths to...
steps.instruction: Open MATLAB and create paths to the folder containing the image sequence to be analyzed (e.g., Image_Sequence.tif ) and folders containing the SLIMfast, DiffusionSingle, TrackValidator, and HMM_Batch MATLAB programs.

steps.title: Use the command run SLIMfast in...
steps.instruction: Use the command run SLIMfast in the MATLAB command window, which will open a graphical user interface (GUI) for SLIMfast ( Figure 3 ), a program that uses a multiple-target tracing (MTT) algorithm to achieve 2D single-molecule localization (x,y) [20].

steps.title: Using the SLIMfast GUI, open the...
steps.instruction: Using the SLIMfast GUI, open the image sequence to be analyzed: Load → Imagestack → Image_Sequence.tif . The GUI will display the first image ( Frame 1 ) in the image sequence. Icons with commands will appear above the Frame 1 image (icon functions described in Figure 3 ).

steps.title: Click on the OPT icon, which...
steps.instruction: Click on the OPT icon, which will open a GUI containing options to modify the imaging conditions and tracking parameters. You can access these parameters using the drop-down menu. First, enter the correct acquisition ( Acquisition ) and localization ( Localization ) parameters, and then close the OPT GUI. Note: See Table 1 for acquisition and localization parameters that should be changed or optimized according to experimental conditions.

steps.title: Press the LOC ALL icon to execute particle localization
steps.instruction: Press the LOC ALL icon to execute particle localization. After pressing the LOC ALL icon, select a folder to save the files containing the localization data. A progress bar will appear.

steps.title: Once localization is complete, the progress bar will disappear
steps.instruction: Once localization is complete, the progress bar will disappear. Close the Frame 1 GUI.

steps.title: Use the drop-down menu to load the particle data
steps.instruction: Use the drop-down menu to load the particle data: Load → Particle Data → SLIMfast → Image_Sequence.rpt . A maximum intensity projection (MIP) of the image sequence will automatically appear.

steps.title: Press the OPT icon
steps.instruction: Press the OPT icon. Change tracking parameters ( Tracking) using the drop-down menu. Optimizing these parameters may be necessary to find the most accurate trajectories. Close the OPT GUI once complete. Notes:

steps.title: Although the default values for most...
steps.instruction: Although the default values for most tracking parameters are generally robust and have been previously described in detail [20, 25–28], two critical parameters should be optimized:

steps.title: the maximum OFF-time
steps.instruction: the maximum OFF-time. The maximum expected diffusion coefficient constrains the step size of particles based on how quickly a particle is predicted to diffuse. The maximum OFF-time determines the number of frames before a trajectory is terminated if the trajectory fails to reconnect (i.e., determines the tolerance for when a particle disappears, either through blinking, bleaching, or because it has moved in Z).

steps.title: See Table 1 for critical tracking...
steps.instruction: See Table 1 for critical tracking parameters that may require optimization.

steps.title: Press the GEN TRAJ icon to generate trajectories
steps.instruction: Press the GEN TRAJ icon to generate trajectories. A progress bar will appear. After the progress bar disappears, save files containing the tracking parameters. Close the SLIMfast GUIs.

steps.title: Return to the MATLAB command window...
steps.instruction: Return to the MATLAB command window and use the command run DiffusionSingle . Open Image_Sequence.rpt_tracked_table.txt.

steps.title: A MATLAB figure will appear, containing...
steps.instruction: A MATLAB figure will appear, containing particle positions over time ( Figure 4A ). Use the cursor to outline the region of interest. DiffusionSingle will then use MSDanalyzer [29] to compute the diffusion coefficients for each trajectory and produce two figures:

steps.title: a distribution of diffusion coefficients ( Figure 4B , C)
steps.instruction: a distribution of diffusion coefficients ( Figure 4B , C). Notes:

steps.title: Access the DiffusionSingle
steps.instruction: Access the DiffusionSingle.m MATLAB file to change the time between images in the timelapse (i.e., the lag time). We use a lag time of 20–50 ms.

steps.title: The fraction of the MSD plot...
steps.instruction: The fraction of the MSD plot used for fitting to determine the diffusion coefficient can be modified inside the DiffusionSingle.m file.

steps.title: Raw data is accessible in the...
steps.instruction: Raw data is accessible in the ma_all MAT file in MATLAB.

steps.title: Return to the MATLAB command window...
steps.instruction: Return to the MATLAB command window and use the command run TrackValidator to open the TrackValidator GUI ( Figure 5 ).

steps.title: Upload image sequence
steps.instruction: Upload image sequence: Loading TIFF file (1) → Image_Sequence.tif.

steps.title: Upload track file
steps.instruction: Upload track file: Loading Track file (2) → Image_Sequence.rpt_tracked_table.

steps.title: Using the Track Navigator icons, inspect each track for accuracy
steps.instruction: Using the Track Navigator icons, inspect each track for accuracy. To examine whether the localization of a particle is correct for every frame, use the Last Spot and Next Spot icons. Use the Delink Track icon to split a trajectory into two trajectories before and after the current frame. This tool is useful when only part of the trajectory is accurate. If a track is accurate, press the Curated icon.

steps.title: Once accurate tracks have been curated,...
steps.instruction: Once accurate tracks have been curated, press the Save Curated (

steps.title: icon and save the MAT file
steps.instruction: icon and save the MAT file. Move this MAT file into a new folder titled Batch . The curated tracks can be reopened using TrackValidator by pressing the Curated Only icon and loading the MAT file containing curated tracks.

steps.title: To determine the motion states and...
steps.instruction: To determine the motion states and parameters using HMM-Bayes [21], use the command run HMM_Batch in the MATLAB command window. When prompted, select the Batch folder containing the MAT file with curated tracks. HMM_Batch will produce and save MATLAB figures containing the motion parameters, state sequences, step-size distributions, and the probability of each motion state for each trajectory ( Figure 6 ). Note: Access the HMM_Batch.m MATLAB file to change the lag time. Figure

steps.title: MATLAB graphical user interface for the SLIMfast program
steps.instruction: MATLAB graphical user interface for the SLIMfast program. SLIMfast is an in-house MATLAB program based on the multiple-target tracing (MTT) algorithm developed by Serge et al. [20] for single-particle tracking. Arrows , cycle through the frames of the timelapse sequence; PLAY MOV , play the timelapse sequence; OPT , opens a graphical user interface (GUI) with options for acquisition, localization, and tracking parameters; MAX PROJ , creates a figure with a maximum intensity projection of the timelapse sequence; AVE PROJ , creates a figure with an average intensity projection of the timelapse sequence; TEST LOC , locates the particles on the current frame of the timelapse sequence; LOC ALL , performs localization on all particles in the timelapse sequence; GEN TRAJ , performs reconnection to generate trajectories. Table

steps.title: Important parameters to be optimized for...
steps.instruction: Important parameters to be optimized for single-particle tracking Acquisition Parameter Description Values in [23] Pixel size (μm) Pixel size used by the camera. 0.16 μm Emission (nm) Emission wavelength of the imaged dye. We used JF 549 for the chase. 571 nm Numerical aperture (N.A.) The numerical aperture of the used objective. We use a 100× Apo TIRF objective. 1.49 Counts/photon Adjust according to camera specifications. 20.2 Lag time (ms) Time interval between images. 50 ms Localization Parameter Description Values in [23] Deflation loops Determines how often the detection process will be iterated. After each iteration, detected particles, represented by their Gaussian intensity profile, will be removed from the image. This allows low-intensity particles to be detected. 3 Tracking Parameter Description Values in [23] Maximum expected diffusion coefficient (μm 2 /s) Constrains the step size of particles based on how quickly a particle is predicted to diffuse. 1 μm 2 /s Maximum number of competitors Determines the maximum number of trajectories that can compete for a particle. 3 Maximum OFF-time The number of frames before a trajectory is terminated if the trajectory fails to reconnect. 1 Figure

steps.title: Example figure outputs for the DiffusionSingle program
steps.instruction: Example figure outputs for the DiffusionSingle program. DiffusionSingle is an in-house MATLAB program that utilizes MSDanalyzer (created by Tarantino et al. [29]) to calculate diffusion coefficients. (A) Overlay of all particles localized in the timelapse sequence. (B) Trajectories reconstructed from the timelapse sequence. (C) Distribution of diffusion coefficients (Log 10 D). Figure

steps.title: MATLAB graphical user interface for the TrackValidator program
steps.instruction: MATLAB graphical user interface for the TrackValidator program. TrackValidator is an in-house MATLAB program for the visualization and curation of trajectories. Using TrackValidator, the motion state of the current trajectory can be determined by pressing the HMM-Bayes analysis icon (HMM) [21]. Alternatively, curated trajectories can be analyzed in batch using the HMM_Batch program. Figure

steps.title: Results generated by HMM-Bayes analysis
steps.instruction: Results generated by HMM-Bayes analysis. HMM-Bayes [21] will generate a figure for each curated trajectory containing

steps.title: the relative xy positions of the particles in a trajectory,
steps.instruction: the relative xy positions of the particles in a trajectory,

steps.title: the probability that the motion state is correctly predicted,
steps.instruction: the probability that the motion state is correctly predicted,

steps.title: a step-size distribution between particles
steps.instruction: a step-size distribution between particles. D, diffusion; DV, active transport. I. GluA1-HaloTag-JF 549 vesicle identification

steps.title: Curate trajectories that exhibit active transport (i
steps.instruction: Curate trajectories that exhibit active transport (i.e., trajectories predicted by HMM-Bayes to exhibit active transport, DV, with high probability; see Figure 6 ).

steps.title: Determine the bleaching rate for GluA1-HT-JF...
steps.instruction: Determine the bleaching rate for GluA1-HT-JF 549 particles to separate single receptors from vesicles. a. Plot the fluorescence intensity over time for a GluA1-HT-JF 549 particle using NIS-Elements or ImageJ. JF 549 will bleach in a stepwise fashion. b. Determine the number of frames between each bleaching step. c. Repeat this analysis for several GluA1-HT-JF 549 particles located at different positions in the dendrite. d. Determine the bleaching step that required the most frames and use this as a threshold for the number of frames required to bleach a single JF 549 particle. e. Multiply this number of frames by

steps.title: This is the maximum number of...
steps.instruction: This is the maximum number of frames required for a GluA1-HT homomeric receptor (i.e., four GluA1 subunits) to be bleached. Filter out trajectories (that exhibit only diffusion, not active transport) that terminate in fewer frames. Under the imaging conditions we previously used, we found that single GluA1-HT-JF 549 homomers bleach in fewer than 100 frames. This analysis should be performed as imaging conditions change.

steps.title: For trajectories with diffusion, filter out...
steps.instruction: For trajectories with diffusion, filter out particles that have a diffusion coefficient greater than 0.45 μm 2 /s and less than 0.02 μm 2 /s. We previously found that particles with very high and low diffusion rates are likely to be single receptors diffusing on the neuronal surface or clusters of receptors trapped inside postsynaptic densities [23].

description: Validation of protocol This protocol or parts of it have been used and validated in the following research articles: Suzuki et al. [17]. In vivo genome editing via CRISPR/Cas9 mediated homology-independent targeted integration. Nature ( Figure 2 ; Extended Data Figure 5 ; Extended Data Figure 7). This research article describes the development and validation of homology-independent targeted integration as a tool to edit genes in post-mitotic cells, including cultured neurons. Grimm et al. [19]. A general method to improve fluorophores for live-cell and single-molecule microscopy. Nature methods. This research article describes the development of JF 646 and JF 549 and their utility in cellular imaging. Wong et al. [23]. Plasticity-induced actin polymerization in the dendritic shaft regulates intracellular AMPA receptor trafficking. eLife ( Figure 1A –D; Figure 1 : Figure Supplement 1–8). This research article describes the validation of the GluA1-HT knock-in and also describes the use of single-particle tracking to characterize the motion of GluA1-HT + vesicles. This includes characterizing the knock-in efficiency of HaloTag into GluA1 and the rate of errors; demonstrating that GluA1-HT functions and is trafficked in a manner similar to untagged GluA1; and characterizing and validating the motion of GluA1-HT + vesicles. Sergé et al. [20]. Robust single-particle tracking in live-cell time-lapse sequences. Nature methods. This research article describes multiple-target tracing (MTT) and its utility in single-particle tracking. Monnier et al. [21]. Inferring transient particle transport dynamics in live cells. Nature methods. This research article describes the development and validation of HMM-Bayes as a tool to characterize the motion states of single particles. Liu et al. [27]. Direct Observation of Compartment-Specific Localization and Dynamics of Voltage-Gated Sodium Channels. J Neurosci (Figure 13). This research article uses both the block-and-chase protocol and
